CSRF/XSRF With a Decoupled Frontend and Backend
CSRF is pretty common and usually solvable, but with a fully decoupled frontend and backend, the approach is different.
Since I described the basics in an earlier post —
Full-stack Warrior's Path Node.js: CSRF (article in Mandarin)
— let me just quote what I wrote then:
CSRF basically exploits the browser's trust in a website to launch the attack. Suppose we already know an API path, e.g. an account-deletion API
. The hacker sends you a message "Hey, click this URL and you'll get a Bitcoin" (yeah, right).
calls out the core concept in bold:
The attack exploits the trust that a site has in a user's browser.
Simple authentication only guarantees the request comes from the user's browser — it doesn't guarantee the user actually intended to make the request.

In the earlier post, I described how to handle CSRF without a decoupled frontend/backend. In short: when the backend renders the page via SSR, it sends a random CSRF Token string to the frontend, stored somewhere on the page (usually a hidden form input). If you open DevTools, you can find this hidden field:
With this approach the backend has to keep a CSRF Token in memory. When the frontend submits, the backend pulls the field out and compares — if they match, the action (create, update, etc.) proceeds.

That works fine without full frontend/backend decoupling. But with full decoupling, things change — we're no longer in the backend environment, the frontend isn't rendered by the backend, so how do we get the CSRF Token? The key idea:
"Generate the CSRF Token in the browser."
At this point you might wonder — doesn't CORS handle this? Or what if we just open a backend API to fetch a CSRF Token?
Think about it — CORS can be bypassed. CORS only really protects XMLHttpRequest. And opening a dedicated CSRF Token API is weird — if I can bypass CORS, I can just keep fetching the token.
Back to the topic — Client Double Submit Cookie. We said "generate the CSRF Token in the browser". One way is to write a random string generator:
XSRF-TOKEN is the same thing as CSRF-TOKEN.
Cross-site request forgery (also known as XSRF or CSRF) is an attack against web-hosted apps
Then when sending a request to the backend, include the XSRF-TOKEN from the cookie. With axios, just configure it like this (axios is really convenient):

axios.defaults.xsrfCookieName: 'XSRF-TOKEN', // Same name as what you stored in cookies — axios defaults to 'XSRF-TOKEN'
axios.defaults.xsrfHeaderName = 'X-XSRF-TOKEN'; // The header name when sending the request — coordinate with the backend, some backends receive 'XSRF-TOKEN'; axios defaults to 'X-XSRF-TOKEN'
// Same name as what you stored in cookies — axios defaults to 'XSRF-TOKEN'
// The header name when sending the request — coordinate with the backend, some backends receive 'XSRF-TOKEN'; axios defaults to 'X-XSRF-TOKEN'
After this, every axios request to the backend automatically carries the client-generated CSRF Token, which prevents CSRF attacks.
Why does this work? Picture what happens when a user clicks this:

The user navigates directly to that URL — no axios involved, no client-generated XSRF-TOKEN. The backend won't perform the delete. To actually delete, you need both a Token and an XSRF-TOKEN, which closes this attack surface.
You might also ask, "But normally a login session already requires a Token to delete, right?"
Right — but adding this extra protection on top reduces the attack surface. This kind of CSRF protection mostly targets pages that don't require a Token.
Let's talk about CSRF (article in Mandarin)
